summary: Gruber critiques Nilay Patel's iPhone 7 review for its obsessive focus on the removed headphone jack, arguing the review will age poorly — much like 2007 reviews that fixated on the original iPhone's lack of a hardware keyboard. He catalogs an extensive list of sentences mentioning 'headphone' from Patel's review to illustrate the point. Gruber also pushes back on Patel's framing of Bluetooth pairing issues as Apple's problem, arguing that Bluetooth itself is the problem and no platform handles it well. He defends Apple's W1 chip advantage as earned rather than anti-competitive, and mocks the logical inconsistency of arguing that removing the headphone jack creates a need for better wireless speaker integration that already existed.
key_points: Patel's review is disproportionately focused on the headphone jack removal, similar to how early iPhone reviews fixated on the missing hardware keyboard
Bluetooth pairing problems are inherent to the Bluetooth standard itself, not something Apple deliberately makes worse to advantage its own products
Apple's custom W1 silicon advantage in wireless audio is earned through hardware investment, and competitors' inability to match it is their problem
The argument that removing the headphone jack creates a new need for wireless speaker integration is logically incoherent — that need existed regardless
Open standards had their chance with wireless audio and failed to deliver a good experience
money_quotes: Nilay's review is going to age about as well as a 2007 review of the original iPhone that devoted the same amount of attention to the lack of a hardware keyboard.
Apple supports completely standard Bluetooth as well as anyone. The problem with Bluetooth isn't Apple. The problem is Bluetooth. It sucks.
There is no open standard for wireless audio that doesn't suck. "Open" had its chance, and they blew it.
Can other companies create their own custom silicon of the size, quality, and power efficiency of the W1? I have no idea, but (a) my hunch says no, and (b) that's their problem, not Apple's.
Are iPhones with headphone jacks somehow in less need of dead-simple integrations with wireless speaker systems?
key_insight: The headphone jack backlash mirrors the hardware keyboard backlash of 2007 — critics are overweighting a temporary inconvenience and underweighting Apple's bet on a wireless future it is uniquely positioned to lead.
